title: 3 2 Vector Fields
body:
3.2 VECTOR FIELDS

    Given a scalar field ֿ†(x, y, z) and a vector field F(x, y, z) = F1 i + F2 j + F3 k,

                        גˆ‚ֿ†    גˆ‚ֿ†    גˆ‚ֿ†
      גˆ‡ֿ† = grad ֿ† =        i+    j+    k.               grad : scalar to vector
                        גˆ‚x    גˆ‚y    גˆ‚z


                          גˆ‚F1 גˆ‚F2 גˆ‚F3
        גˆ‡ ֲ· F = div F =      +    +    .                div : vector to scalar
                          גˆ‚x   גˆ‚y   גˆ‚z


                              i    j    k
                             גˆ‚     גˆ‚    גˆ‚
       גˆ‡ ֳ— F = curl F =      גˆ‚x    גˆ‚y   גˆ‚z              curl : vector to vector
                             F1 F2 F3

                 where the vector differential operator גˆ‡ is given by
                                        גˆ‚     גˆ‚    גˆ‚
                                   גˆ‡=      i+    j+ k
                                        גˆ‚x    גˆ‚y   גˆ‚z


We turn now to the theory of vector fields, a topic of immense importance in Engineering.
We have seen that a scalar field assigns a scalar to each point in space. For example, the
temperature at each point in the room is a scalar field. In a similar way, a vector field
assigns a vector to each point in space.

An example of a vector field is the air currents in the room. We could imagine measuring
the wind velocity at every point in space. The direction of the wind at a particular point
would then correspond to the direction of the vector at that point, and the magnitude of
the wind would correspond to the length of the vector. There would be large vectors near
AC ducts and small vectors in the corners of the room where there is little air movement.
In fact, vector fields are the ideal tool for studying flows in real-world applications.

Another example of a vector field is the gradient of a scalar field גˆ‡ֿ†, which tells us the
direction and magnitude of maximum increase of the scalar field ֿ†. It is important to
remember that each vector in a vector field is assigned to a particular point in space.
They are definitely not stuck with their base at the origin.

Example 1 Consider the vector field F(x, y, z) = (x2 + y 2 )i + xyzj גˆ’ ex zk. Find the
vector at the point P (0, 2, 3).




                                                                                ן£«
                                                                                ן£¶

                                                                F 4i גˆ’ 3k = ן£­ 0 ן£¸         F
                                                                             גˆ’3

                                               1
 Example 2 Consider the vector field T(x, y) = (x + y)i + (x2 + y 2 + 1)j in R2 . Sketch
the vectors of the field at the set of points {(0, 0), (1, 1), (גˆ’1, 1), (1, גˆ’1), (גˆ’1, גˆ’1)}.




                                                                                      F


                                            2
                Figure 1: Example 2: T(x, y) = (x + y)i + (x2 + y 2 + 1)j.


Remember that vector fields are an attempt to mathematically analyse flows. An example
of a more complicated vector field comes from Example 1.




              Figure 2: Example 1: F(x, y, z) = (x2 + y 2 )i + xyzj גˆ’ ex zk.




                                            3
 Clearly we need some new tools to understand and interpret vector fields! In the rest of
this lecture we will introduce the gradient, divergence and curl ג€” or grad, div, curl for
short. These operations transform vector fields into scalar fields and vice versa. In this
lecture we will look at how these calculations are performed. In the next lecture we will
discuss their meaning.

Example 3 Let ֿ†(x, y, z) = x2 y 3 z + 2y     be a scalar field. Calculate grad(ֿ†).


                         גˆ‚ֿ†    גˆ‚ֿ†    גˆ‚ֿ†
        גˆ‡ֿ† = grad ֿ† =       i+    j+    k.              grad : scalar to vector
                         גˆ‚x    גˆ‚y    גˆ‚z
We are already familiar with the gradient of a scalar field but now we will introduce it in
a slightly different way using the differential operator
                                      גˆ‚     גˆ‚     גˆ‚
                                 גˆ‡=      i+    j + k.
                                      גˆ‚x    גˆ‚y    גˆ‚z
We call this operator del or nabla. Notice that גˆ‡ looks a lot like a vector, except that
its components are the partial derivatives rather than numbers or functions.




                                                                     ן£«               ן£¶

                            F 2xy 3 zi + (3x2 y 2 z + 2)j + x2 y 3 k = ן£­3x2 y 2 z + 2ן£¸   F
                                                                           x2 y 3

 Example 4 Let F(x, y, z) = (x2 + y 2 )i + xyzj גˆ’ ex zk = F1 i + F2 j + F3 k be a vector
field. Find the divergence of the vector field F


                           גˆ‚F1 גˆ‚F2 גˆ‚F3
         גˆ‡ ֲ· F = div F =      +    +    .             div : vector to scalar
                           גˆ‚x   גˆ‚y   גˆ‚z

Again, notice that we can define div using the differential operator גˆ‡. In this case, the
divergence is just the dot product of גˆ‡ and F




                                                                F 2x + xz גˆ’ ex      F

 Example 5 Given the vector field f(x, y, z) = f1 i + f2 j + f3 k = 3yi + xzj גˆ’ x3 k find
curl(f).

                                 f1 f2 f3


For curl we take the cross product of גˆ‡ and F and thus we need to find a determinant.
